In years past, the ABAP breakdown was closer to a 50-50 mix between older, "ABAP Standard" and "ABAP Cloud". This year we see a marked increase for ABAP Cloud (almost 60% total). 
 
CAP/JavaScript is plainly most popular for CAP projects. Consistent with last year, we do have more respondents reporting the use of pure CAP/Java projects, although the percentage is a modest 8 percent.
 
Which Integrated Development Environments are Developer Using
We were curious which Integrated Development Environments are most familiar to our respondents.
 
IDE Familiarity
"Which of these IDEs are are you comfortable working with? (select all that apply)"  

BTP Guidance Framework

Awareness and Use of the BTP Guidance Framework
"The BTP Guidance Framework is intended to help architects, developers, and administrators design, build, and run solutions on the SAP Business Technology Platform. Which statement best described your level of familiarity with this document?"  

 

Nearly half of respondents are still unaware of this document's release. Raising awareness for this and other related BTP framework documents would be a good goal for 2026.

Cloud Insights

We first asked respondents if they used any cloud providers for their projects. For those that answered, "yes", we asked about the use of several major providers, including SAP BTP. We also asked if their use included SAP or non-SAP projects. This information was condensed into a chart depicting the relative use of each cloud provider.

Similar to the earlier questions around BTP Environments, these numbers do not reflect a tally of projects for each platform - instead it reflects developer exposure to each.

 
Using Cloud Providers?
"Are you using any cloud providers for your development projects?" 
Relative Use of Cloud Providers
We asked, "Which cloud providers are you using for development projects?"
 
SAP Business Technology Platform
Microsoft Azure
Amazon Web Services
Google Cloud Platform
Alibaba Cloud

Low Code / No Code Tools

This is the fourth consecutive year in which we've asked questions around Low Code and No Code (LCNC) products. The list was composed of leaders in the then current Gartner LCNC Magic Quadrant Report and select SAP products.

For each product, we asked respondents to select the most applicable category reflecting their awareness or use. These questions were organized as a single large matrix of boxes in Qualtrics - There is no "I am unfamiliar with this product" response. Instead, the respondent simply would skip checking one of the other boxes.

We normalized these data over the reporting years to account for differences in total responses each year. We then tallied production use of each tool in the table below, ordering by use for the current year. Note that respondents may have deployed more that just one product each year, so column totals will not necessarily add up to 100%.

|   Note: Figures for 2024–2025 differ from last year's report due to a correction to the original formula; relative rankings were unaffected.

 

Tool Name 2024% 2025% 2026%
Tool Name 2024% 2025%2026%
SAP Build low code55.061.467.6
Vendor A35.035.741.2
Vendor B20.027.114.7
Vendor Xnana11.8
'na' - not part that year's survey   

SAP Build low code continues to dominate our SAP ecosystem.

Learning and Help Resources

We were interested in preferences in the format or media type of learning resources by respondents.

 
Preferred Learning Resources
"When learning new skills or technical subjects over the past 12 months, what resources did you tend use the most? Please select up to three resources."  
 

These results seem relatively unchanged year-to-year. One point of interest continuing from last year is the large difference in popularity between tutorials and MOOCs (34% versus 8% for 2026). Tutorials tend to be hands-on. They are also broken into substantially smaller time chunks. This reinforces our hypothesis that different content types and required time investment have an impact in some learning scenarios.

On the topic of Help, we shifted in this next question from formats to specific web sites.

 
Top SAP Help Resource Sites
"What is your preferred resource if you need help, or have a challenge with the SAP technology / tool you are working with? "  
We added "Consulting an LLM ..." this year and -- interestingly -- it comes in third place. Although we consider "Help" different than "Learning", this is still an interesting point when it comes to describing LLM's roles in assisting Developers.

SAP Discovery Center Awareness and Use

SAP Discovery Center is a rich resource for helping teams understand how common business use cases map to technical architectures. Other important aspects of SAP BTP use are available there as well. How well know is it outside of SAP and, for folks that do know about it, how often do they use it?

 
BTP Discovery Center
"SAP Discovery Center helps you discover, evaluate, and adopt SAP BTP services and AI features through missions, a detailed service catalog, reference architectures and more. Which of the following statements best describes your level of familiarity with SAP Discovery Center?"  

Technical Certifications

Technical Certifications
"How many technology-related certification programs have you completed within the past four years?"  
 
 

This question is composed to apply to all technology certifications, not just SAP's. But we can see that well over a third (36%) of respondents took no certifications in the past four years. We chose "four years" on purpose. It's designed to be a long period - one that would allow most technical skills to become stale. If the respondent valued these as a means to truly stay current, we reason they'd be likely to re/certify.

For roughly a third of folks, that's not the case.

 
SAP Technical Certifications
"Of these certifications, how many were from SAP?"  
 
This was a follow-up question for those that did take at least one certification. It does appear to indicated that -- for those taking certifications -- that SAP's are among those acquired.

Survey Methodology

This report is based on a Qualtrics web-based survey of 589 respondents from 44 countries. The survey ran for six calendar weeks. Ninety percent of the respondents invested ten minutes or less with the survey.

The survey was promoted via the SAP Community website and the SAP Developer Center, sap.com pop-up intercepts, social media posts.

This Report

This is the fourth year that this report is published as a Docusaurus web application. Docusaurus is a Node-based documentation publishing system. Recharts and venn.js were used to generate custom charts from survey data exported from Qualtrics and anonymized.

Survey results are presented by topic area and are filtered based on question relevance and survey logic. While the survey received 589 total responses, not every respondent saw or answered every question. Some questions were shown only to relevant audiences based on earlier responses, and some allowed multiple selections. As a result, totals and percentages may vary across sections and should be interpreted within the context of each chart. For example, a section focused on AI adoption may reflect only respondents who answered AI-related questions, while Developer Experience and Learning may include broader audiences. This structure helps ensure that each section reflects the most relevant responses, but it also means that sample sizes and percentages can vary throughout the report.

The sections in this report provide a directional view of the SAP developer community in 2026, highlighting key patterns in developer roles, tools, learning habits, and technology adoption. In sections such as Developer Experience, the report focuses on workflows, environments, and friction points in day-to-day development. In Learning, it highlights how developers build skills, with strong reliance on tutorials, documentation, forums, and community resources. For example, the learning section shows that developers most often relied on developer community websites and forums (60%), online tutorials, how-to videos, or webinars (47%), and self-directed learning using documentation and sample code (41%).

Other References

The annual Stack Overflow Developer Survey is an excellent (and no-cost) reference for industry-wide habits of developers.

SlashData's Developer Nation Survey is also a good non-SAP resource.

entry.title: Fullstack CAP Application with HANA Cloud (Decoupled Architecture) [Part-4]
entry.content:
Connecting CAP application to HANA Cloud and Deploying  to Cloud Foundry

In Part 3 , we created the SAP HANA Cloud instance (SHC_DB) on BTP and mapped it to our Cloud Foundry space.

In this Part, we will:

- Update package.json with the correct CDS profiles for each environment
- Run cds add hana to wire the CAP project for HANA Cloud deployment
- Build the MTA archive using mbt build
- Deploy the CAP backend to Cloud Foundry using cf deploy
- Verify the deployment and explore the HANA tables in DB Explorer
- Run the app locally in hybrid mode — connected to the deployed HANA instance

PREREQUISITES

Make sure the following tools are installed before proceeding:

- Cloud Foundry CLI (cf) — logged in to your BTP CF environment: cf login
- MBT (Multi-Target Application Build Tool): npm install -g mbt
- @sap/cds-dk (already installed from Part 1)


STEP 1 — UPDATE PACKAGE.JSON WITH CDS PROFILES

The package.json in the CAP project already has a "cds" section that defines how the app connects to the database in each environment. We need to make sure the three profiles are correctly configured:

development → uses SQLite (local, in-file database — for cds watch)
hybrid → uses SAP HANA Cloud (local app, cloud database — for testing)
production → uses SAP HANA Cloud (fully deployed on CF)

Update or verify the "cds" section in your package.json:

{
  "name": "incident-management",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "description": "Incident Management CAP Application",
  "repository": "<Add your repository here>",
  "license": "UNLICENSED",
  "private": true,
  "dependencies": {
    "@cap-js/hana": "^2",
    "@sap/cds": "^9",
    "@sap/cds-dk": "^9",
    "@sap/xssec": "^4",
    "express": "^5"
  },
  "devDependencies": {
    "@cap-js/sqlite": "^2",
    "@sap/cds-dk": "^9",
    "cds-plugin-ui5": "^0.17.0"
  },
  "cds": {
    "requires": {
      "[development]": {
        "auth": {
          "kind": "dummy"
        },
        "db": {
          "kind": "sqlite"
        }
      },
      "[hybrid]": {
        "auth": {
          "kind": "dummy"
        },
        "db": {
          "kind": "hana-cloud"
        }
      },
      "[production]": {
        "auth": {
          "kind": "dummy"
        },
        "db": {
          "kind": "hana-cloud"
        }
      }
    }
  },
  "scripts": {
    "start": "cds-serve",
    "watch": "cds watch",
    "watch-incidents": "cds watch --open incidents/index.html?sap-ui-xx-viewCache=false --livereload false"
  }
}

WHY THREE PROFILES?
This pattern lets you use the same codebase across all environments without changing any code — just switch the profile.

SQLite keeps local development fast and offline.

Hybrid lets you test against real HANA data without a full CF deployment.

Production is used when the app runs on Cloud Foundry.

 

STEP 2 — RUN cds add hana

Run the following command in the terminal from inside the project folder:

cds add hana

This single command does several important things to prepare your CAP project for SAP HANA Cloud deployment:

- Adds the @cap-js/hana dependency to package.json (HANA database adapter)
- Generates or updates the mta.yaml file with the correct MTA module definitions for CF deployment
- Adds an HDI container resource definition to mta.yaml
- Configures the production CDS profile to use hana-cloud as the db kind

After running this command, your mta.yaml will define three components:

1. incident-management-srv (Node.js module — the CAP OData service)
- Deployed as a CF application
- Provides: srv-api (the service URL consumed by the Fiori app later)
- Requires: incident-management-db (the HDI container)

2. incident-management-db-deployer (hdb module — the schema deployer)
- A one-time deployer app that pushes the CDS schema to HANA
- Runs during deployment then stops (Stopped state is normal)
- Requires: incident-management-db

3. incident-management-db (resource — the HDI container)
- Type: com.sap.xs.hdi-container
- Service: hana, Plan: hdi-shared
- This is the actual schema container created inside your HANA Cloud instance (SHC_DB) that was set up in Part 3 


STEP 3 — BUILD THE MTA ARCHIVE

Run the MBT build command to compile the project into a deployable archive:

mbt build

This command:
- Reads the mta.yaml configuration
- Runs `npm install` and `npx cds build --production` to compile the CDS model
- Packages all modules into a single .mtar archive file

The output archive is created at:

mta_archives/incident-management_1.0.0.mtar

 

STEP 4 — DEPLOY TO CLOUD FOUNDRY

Make sure you are logged in to CF and targeting the correct space:

cf login -a <api endpoint>
cf target -o <your-org> -s dev

Now deploy the MTA archive:

cf deploy mta_archives/incident-management_1.0.0.mtar

CF will:
1. Create the HDI container service instance (incident-management-db)
2. Deploy and run the db-deployer to push the schema to HANA
3. Start the CAP service application (incident-management-srv)

Once deployment completes, navigate to your CF space in BTP Cockpit:

Trial Home →   f030ff54trial   → trial   → dev → Applications

You will see two applications in the "dev" space:

 

STEP 5 — VERIFY THE HDI CONTAINER INSTANCE

Navigate to:

Space: dev → Services → Instances

You should see the HDI container service instance that was automatically created during deployment:

 This HDI container lives inside the SHC_DB HANA Cloud instance we created in Part 3. CAP automatically created it during the cf deploy step.

 

STEP 6 — EXPLORE THE HANA TABLES IN DB EXPLORER

Now let's verify the schema was deployed correctly by exploring the tables in SAP HANA Database Explorer.

Open HANA Cloud Central → click "Open" on the SHC_DB instance →
select "SAP HANA Database Explorer".

In Database Explorer, the left panel shows your SHC-DB instance connected as DBADMIN. To view the HDI container tables, click the "+" (Add Instance) icon.

 

Select "HDI Container" as the Instance Type, filter by your org (f030ff54trial) and space (dev).

Select "incident-management-db" and click OK.

 

The HDI container is now added to the left panel. Expand it to see the Tables folder. You will find all the CDS entities have been deployed as HANA tables.

Click on SAP_CAPIRE_INCIDENTS_INCIDENTS to inspect its structure:


STEP 7 — RUNNING IN HYBRID MODE

Now that the CAP app is deployed and the HDI container exists in BTP, you can run your local development server connected directly to the cloud HANA database. This is the "hybrid" mode — your app runs locally but reads and writes real HANA Cloud data.

This is very useful for testing local code changes against production data before deploying again.

Step 1 — Bind your local project to the HDI container service instance:

cds bind -2 incident-management-db:key

What this does:
- Connects to Cloud Foundry and retrieves the service key for the "incident-management-db" HDI container instance
- Creates a service key on BTP (if one doesn't exist)
- Saves the HANA connection credentials (host, schema, user, password) into .cdsrc-private.json under the "hybrid" profile
- The .cdsrc-private.json file is local only — never commit it to git

Step 2 — Start the server in hybrid mode:

cds watch --profile hybrid

The terminal confirms the app is now connected to the real HANA Cloud database, showing:
- The HANA host and schema ID
- Driver: com.sap.db.jdbc.Driver (JDBC connection to HANA)
- Both ProcessorService and AdminService are being served

Your app on localhost:4004 now reads and writes directly to the HANA Cloud instance in BTP — the Fiori preview will show and save real data.


SUMMARY

In Part 4, we successfully:

✔ Configured package.json with three CDS profiles: development (SQLite), hybrid (local + HANA Cloud), production (CF + HANA)
✔ Ran cds add hana to wire the project for HANA Cloud and generate mta.yaml
✔ Built the deployable MTA archive using mbt build
✔ Deployed the CAP backend to Cloud Foundry using cf deploy
✔ Verified the two CF apps: incident-management-srv (Started) and incident-management-db-deployer (Stopped — expected)
✔ Confirmed the HDI container instance was created with 2 bindings
✔ Explored the HANA tables and seed data in SAP HANA Database Explorer
✔ Connected the local app to the HANA Cloud instance using hybrid mode

The CAP backend is now live on Cloud Foundry and backed by SAP HANA Cloud.
In Part 5 , we will build the Fiori Elements UI application and connect it to this deployed CAP service.

entry.title: Fullstack CAP Application with HANA Cloud (Decoupled Architecture) [Part-6]
entry.content:
Building and Deploying the Fiori App to HTML5 Repository

In the previous parts we:

✔ Built and tested the CAP backend locally (Part 1  & Part 2 )
✔ Set up SAP HANA Cloud on BTP (Part 3 )
✔ Deployed the CAP backend to Cloud Foundry (Part 4 )
✔ Configured the BTP Destination to point to the CAP service (Part 5 )

In this Part, we will:
- Generate the Fiori Elements app using the SAP Fiori Application Generator
- Add deployment configuration for Cloud Foundry and the HTML5 Repository
- Build and deploy the Fiori app as a standalone HTML5 application
- Access the fully working end-to-end application via SAP Build Work Zone

 

PREREQUISITES

Before proceeding, make sure the following are in place:

- VS Code with the SAP Fiori Tools Extension Pack installed / Business Application Studio (BAS)
- Cloud Foundry CLI logged in (cf login)
- The CAP backend deployed and running (from Part 4)
- The destination "destination-trial" configured in BTP (from Part 5)

> IMPORTANT — You also need the following active subscriptions in your BTP Subaccount before you can access the deployed Fiori app:
> - SAP HTML5 Application Repository Service
> - SAP Cloud Identity Services
> - SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition (for the Launchpad)

> Follow the step-by-step guide here  to enable these.

 

PART A — DEVELOPING THE FIORI UI APP

STEP 1 — OPEN THE SAP FIORI APPLICATION GENERATOR

In VS Code/BAS, press Ctrl+Shift+P and search for: SAP Fiori: Open Application Generator

The SAP Fiori Generator panel opens with a multi-step wizard on the left.

 

STEP 2 — SELECT THE TEMPLATE

On the Template Selection screen, choose: List Report Page

This template generates a two-page Fiori Elements app — a List Report for browsing records and an Object Page for viewing/editing individual records.
It uses the CDS Annotations we defined in Part 2 to render the UI automatically.

 Click "Next >".

 

STEP 3 — DATA SOURCE AND SERVICE SELECTION

Data Source:  Connect to an OData Service

 Click "Next >". The generator fetches the metadata from the live service.

STEP 4 — ENTITY SELECTION

Once the metadata is loaded, select:

Main Entity: Incidents
Navigation Entity: None
Generate Annotations: Yes
Table Type: Responsive

The generator will pick up the CDS Annotations we defined in Part 2 automatically since "Generate Annotations" is set to Yes.

Click "Next >".

STEP 5 — PROJECT ATTRIBUTES

Fill in the project details:

 Click "Finish". The generator scaffolds the complete Fiori Elements project in the specified folder. This creates the "incidentmanagement" app folder with webapp/, manifest.json, xs-app.json, and all config files.

STEP 6 — RUN AND TEST APP LOCALLY

npm run start-noflp

 

PART B — DEPLOYING THE FIORI APP TO HTML5 REPOSITORY

STEP 7 — ADD DEPLOYMENT CONFIGURATION

Navigate into the Fiori app folder and run:

npm run deploy-config

This command launches an interactive prompt to configure the deployment.


Answer the prompts as follows:

Target environment: Cloud Foundry
Destination name: destination-trial
App Router: Managed Application Router

Running this command generates/updates two key files:

mta.yaml → Multi-Target Application descriptor for CF deployment.
Defines three CF resources to be created:
- incidentmanagement-destination-service (destination, lite)→ includes a built-in "ui5" destination for SAPUI5 resources
- incidentmanagement-uaa (XSUAA, application plan)→ OAuth2 security for the app, configured via xs-security.json
- incidentmanagement-repo-host (html5-apps-repo, app-host)→ the HTML5 Application Repository bucket that stores the app

ui5-deploy.yaml → UI5 build configuration.
Packages the app into incidentmanagement.zip (excluding test/ and localService/ folders) and bundles xs-app.json into the archive for AppRouter routing rules.

 

STEP 8 — BUILD THE MTA ARCHIVE

Run the build command:

npm run build:mta

This command:
- Runs npm install and npm run build:cf to compile the UI5 application
- Packages the compiled app into incidentmanagement.zip
- Bundles everything into a deployable MTA archive: mta_archives/incidentmanagement_0.0.1.mtar

 

STEP 9 — DEPLOY TO CLOUD FOUNDRY

Deploy the Fiori app MTA archive to Cloud Foundry:

cf deploy mta_archives/incidentmanagement_0.0.1.mtar

During deployment, CF will automatically:
1. Create the XSUAA service instance (incidentmanagement-xsuaa-service)
2. Create the HTML5 App Repository host (incidentmanagement-html5-service)
3. Create the Destination service instance (incidentmanagement-destination-service)— including the built-in "ui5" destination for SAPUI5 CDN resources
4. Uploads the Fiori app zip to the HTML5 Repository
5. Register the app's destination content (html5 repo host + XSUAA token exchange)

STEP 10 — VERIFY AND ACCESS LIVE APPLICATION IN HTML5 REPO

In the BTP Cockpit, navigate to your Subaccount and click:

HTML5 Applications (in the left navigation)

You will see the deployed Fiori app listed under the Managed Application Router provided by SAP Build Work Zone:

 

Click on "incidentmanagement" in the HTML5 Applications page to open the app in SAP Build Work Zone Launchpad.

The Fiori Elements List Report opens in the browser — now running fully in the cloud, reading live data from SAP HANA Cloud via the CAP service:

 
SERIES SUMMARY:

Over 6 parts, we built and deployed a complete, production-grade Incident Management application on SAP BTP using modern SAP technologies:

Part 1  → CAP Node.js project with domain model, OData services,CSV seed data — running locally on SQLite

Part 2  → CDS Annotations for the Fiori Elements UI:field labels, list/object page layout, value helps

Part 3  → SAP HANA Cloud instance (SHC_DB) on BTP — entitlements,roles, instance creation, CF environment mapping

Part 4  → CAP backend deployed to Cloud Foundry, HDI containercreated, schema deployed, hybrid mode with cds bind

Part 5  → BTP Destination (destination-trial) configured to bridge the Fiori app to the CAP service URL

Part 6 (This Part) → Fiori Elements app generated, configured for HTML5 Repo, deployed to Cloud Foundry — live end-to-end on BTP

The result is a fully decoupled architecture where the CAP backend and the Fiori frontend are deployed and scaled independently a pattern recommended by SAP for modern BTP applications.

I hope this series helped you understand how CAP, HANA Cloud, Fiori Elements, and BTP services come together to build a real application.

 In the coming parts, we will get to know about configuring the App Router, understanding xs-security.json, adding validations, implementing custom actions, securing our app with XSUAA, and even more. Stay tuned!

entry.title: Adding Immutable Blockchain Proofs to SAP With CAP - Cardano and OData V4
entry.content:
ODATANO: Turning Cardano into a CAP Plugin

Hi everyone. I'm Max, a SAP developer for 10 years. In my spare time, I've been experimenting with Blockchain and immutable data proofs a lot lately. In particular, with Cardano, because it's one of the few public blockchains built on peer-reviewed research and formal methods, which matters a lot when you're thinking about anchoring any kind of enterprise data. So I tried to work out a way to bring the two worlds together.

This is a write-up of what came out of it. I hope this is useful. Feedback and pushbacks are very welcome.

Why is blockchain next to SAP at all

SAP systems are trusted but mutable. An admin can edit records, a migration can rewrite values, and an audit trail living in the same database as the data it tracks is only as trustworthy as the permissions around it.

A public blockchain solves that one specific problem: immutable, externally verifiable proof that a piece of data existed at a given moment, outside any one organization's trust boundary.

The use cases aren't exotic at all. Audit anchoring, batch traceability, cross-company verification, and regulatory compliance are just a few. So the hard part isn't the use case. It's the integration. Chain-specific SDKs, raw cryptographic payloads, wallet protocols, and provider APIs have nothing in common with the OData services that SAP Systems are used to consuming.

As I got more familiar with CAP, I thought to myself, what if the blockchain were just another data source behind a CAP service?

The CAP reflex

Anyone who's worked with CAP knows the regular pattern. Model the domain in CDS, write service handlers, let the framework handle OData, routing, metadata, and authentication.

Cardano's domain maps surprisingly cleanly to CDS. Blocks, Transactions, UTxOs, Assets, Stake Pools, Epochs are all typed entities with relationships. A Cardano transaction, for example, always has an input and output composition. And one Output has a composition of different Assets. An Asset has policyId, assetName, fingerprint, and quantity. Conceptually, no further from SAP than, say, an external material service with detailed classifications.

If the domain can be modeled, the rest can too.

What ODATANO is

ODATANO is a CAP application that exposes Cardano as three OData V4 services. More importantly, it's packaged as a CAP plugin (@odatano/core on npm). If you've used @cap-js/sqlite or @SAP/cds-hana,  You know the drill: install the package, add a config block, plugin self-registers at startup.

No custom middleware. No manual route wiring. No code changes in your host app.

Setup

npm install @odatano/core @cap-js/sqlite

An example of the “cds” section in a package.jsonconsumer project might look like this:

 
"cds": {
    "requires": {
      "[development]": {
        "auth": { "kind": "dummy"}
      },
      "db": {
        "kind": "sqlite",
        "credentials": { "url": "db.sqlite" }
      },
      "odatano-core": {
        "network": "preview",
        "backends": [ "koios" ],
        "txBuilders": [ "buildooor" ]
      }
    }
  }

The plugin auto-registers and is available after

cds deploy --to sqlite && cds watch

Three OData services are now live alongside your own, same CAP runtime, same auth middleware, full $metadata discovery.

The three services

Read (/odata/v4/cardano-odata) 18 entities: Blocks, Transactions, Addresses, UTxOs, Assets, Stake Pools, Epochs, Accounts, DReps, Protocol Parameters. Full OData V4 query surface: $filter, $select, $expand, $orderby, $top, $skip, $count. 

Transaction (/odata/v4/cardano-transaction) 11 actions covering the full Build/Sign/Submit lifecycle. ADA transfers, multi-asset, native token minting, CIP-20 metadata, Plutus V3. Each action returns a TransactionBuilds entity with fee estimate, unsigned CBOR, and outputs.

Signing (/odata/v4/cardano-sign) 8 actions implementing external signing as a state machine (pending → verified → submitted). CIP-30 wallets, Cardano CLI, PKCS#11 HSMs. The service never touches private keys.

Total: 31 entities, 34 actions, three services.

Why OData & typed entities matter

Every Cardano entity is a proper CDS model with associations and compositions. That changes what consumers can do:

GET /odata/v4/cardano-odata/Transactions('abc123')?$expand=inputs,outputs($expand=assets)

Response is structured and typed. Fiori, Excel, Power BI, custom consumers, nobody has to parse raw blockchain data.

Same principle for actions. Every parameter has a name, type, and annotations. API Business Hub and Gateway Client render input forms from the $metadata document alone.

CAP Architecture patterns

I tried not to invent much. Where CAP had a pattern, I used it.

Service handlers wrap business logic in handleRequest(), which catches blockchain-specific errors and maps them to OData responses. For example, 400 for validation, 404 for missing UTxOs, 503 for backend outages, so service consumers never see a raw chain exception.

Lazy on-demand indexing. No bulk replication. First query for a block, transaction, or address fetches from the backend, persists viaUPSERT in the Database, and serves subsequent requests from cache until TTL expires.

Multi-backend failover. Three backends (Blockfrost, Koios, Ogmios). The orchestrator picks the right one per request type (Ogmios for live UTxOs, Blockfrost for historical) and fails over on timeout. Transparent to the consumer.

Database-agnostic. CDS entities and standard CAP persistence. SQLite locally, HANA on BTP. No code change, no schema migration. CDS compiler handles the DDL.

TRACE: a concrete use case

To make this less abstract and to test the plugin firsthand, I built a second CAP app: TRACE, a Fiori frontend for pharmaceutical supply chain tracking. TRACE just consumes @odatano/core as a plugin and can use all of the available services for interacting with the Cardano blockchain without having to write one line of custom blockchain code.

Business Scenario: A manufacturer produces a batch. Regulation requires tamper-proof evidence of every custody transfer from the manufacturer to the distributor to the pharmacy. Nobody can rewrite history afterward.

The manufacturer mints the batch as an NFT via ODATANOs BuildMintTransaction OData Action. The NFT carries an inline datum (manufacturer ID, batch ID, custody state) written on-chain at creation.

Each Batch handover calls the OData Action BuildPlutusSpendTransaction. The Plutus V3 Cardano Smartcontract validator enforces on-chain who's allowed to transfer. inlineDatumJson carries the updated custody state to the continuing output.

Any party, manufacturer, regulator, pharmacy, or end consumer can read the full custody chain without access to anyone's SAP system. Because the data lives on Cardano, not in any vendor database.

Conclusion

ODATANO started as a personal question: could a public Blockchain like Cardano, with smart contracts & transaction logic, feel like another OData Service? After half a year of building, I think the answer is yes. Three OData services, familiar CAP patterns, and TRACE as proof that a real consumer app doesn't need a single line of blockchain-specific code.  What excites me most is that this way, Cardano just shows up as another service in the stack, with the same $metadata, the same query syntax, the same handlers as any other CAP service, just with tamper-proof storage and cryptographic guarantees by design.

Status

Funded by Cardano Catalyst Fund 14 (Open Developers). Apache 2.0. Final milestone approved late April, no reviewer refusals, about two months before the deadline.

  • Version: v1.7.8 (@odatano/core on npm)
  • Tests: 1,285 tests across 31 suites, 97% statement coverage
  • Cardano Networks: mainnet, preview, preprod
  • Backends: Blockfrost, Koios, Ogmios (with a real Cardano node)
  • CAP compatibility: @SAP/cds 9.x, Node.js 18+
  • Main Repo: github.com/ODATANO/ODATANO
  • TRACE (Example Consumer App):  TRACE 
  • Homepage: https://odatano.dev/ 

If anyone wants to poke at it, try it, tear it apart, please do. I'm especially interested in feedback from people deep in CAP, because I'm never quite sure which patterns are the most idiomatic. Issues, threads, and messages are all welcome.

Thanks and best regards

Max

entry.title: Fullstack CAP Application with HANA Cloud (Decoupled Architecture) [Part-5]
entry.content:
Configuring the Destination in BTP

In Part 4 , we deployed the CAP backend to Cloud Foundry. It is now live and accessible via a public CF route URL.

In this Part, we will configure a BTP Destination — a named connection entry that acts as a pointer from BTP services to the deployed CAP backend URL.

This destination is the critical link between the Fiori Elements app (which we will deploy to the HTML5 Application Repository in Part 6) and the CAP OData service running on Cloud Foundry.


WHY IS A DESTINATION NEEDED?

In a decoupled architecture, the Fiori app and the CAP backend are deployed independently:

Fiori App (HTML5 Repo) ──► xs-app.json routes ──► Destination ──► CAP Service (CF)

When the browser loads the Fiori app and makes OData calls (e.g., GET /odata/v4/incidents-processor/Incidents), those requests are routed through the AppRouter via xs-app.json. The routing rule in xs-app.json says:

Any request matching ^/odata/(.*) → forward to "destination-trial"

The destination named "destination-trial" then resolves to the actual HTTPS URL of the CAP service on Cloud Foundry. This way, the Fiori app never needs to hardcode the backend URL — it just uses the destination name.

 

STEP 1 — NAVIGATE TO DESTINATIONS IN BTP COCKPIT

In the SAP BTP Cockpit, navigate to your Subaccount and go to:

Connectivity → Destinations

The Destinations page will open showing an empty list (All: 0) since no destinations have been created yet.

The left panel shows the context level:
trial - Destinations → trial (Read and Write access)

 

STEP 2 — CREATE A NEW DESTINATION

Click the "Create" button. A small dialog appears with three options:

From Scratch → Create a new destination manually by filling in the form From File → Import a destination from a previously exported config file Service Instance → Auto-create a destination from a BTP service instance

Select "From Scratch" since we will fill in the details manually.

 

STEP 3 — FIND THE CAP SERVICE URL

Before filling in the destination form, you need the URL of the deployed CAP service. Navigate to:

Space: dev → Applications → incident-management-srv → Application Overview

In the "Mapped Routes" section on the right, you will see the public CF route assigned to the CAP service.

  Copy this route URL — you will paste it as the destination URL in the next step.

STEP 4 — FILL IN THE DESTINATION DETAILS

Back on the Destinations page, fill in the form.

 

 

 WHY NoAuthentication?
In this tutorial setup with a trial account, we are using "dummy" auth in the CAP service (as configured in package.json).

For a productive setup, you would use OAuth2ClientCredentials or OAuth2UserTokenExchange with an XSUAA service instance to secure the backend properly.

Click "Save" to create the destination.

The destination is now listed in the Destinations page:

Name: destination-trial | Type: HTTP

HOW THE DESTINATION IS USED IN THE FIORI APP

The Fiori app references this destination by name in two key files:

xs-app.json (AppRouter routing rules):
- Any request to /odata/* is forwarded to "destination-trial"
- This routes all OData calls from the Fiori UI to the CAP service
- Authentication is handled via XSUAA

manifest.json (OData data source):
- The mainService data source points to /odata/v4/incidents-processor/
- This path matches the /odata/* routing rule in xs-app.json, so it will automatically be forwarded to the destination-trial destination and reach the CAP ProcessorService on Cloud Foundry

> In short: Fiori app calls /odata/v4/incidents-processor/Incidents
> → xs-app.json routes it to destination-trial
> → destination-trial resolves to the CF route of incident-management-srv
> → CAP service responds with HANA Cloud data


SUMMARY

In Part 5, we successfully:

✔ Navigated to the Destinations section in the BTP Subaccount
✔ Created a new HTTP destination named "destination-trial" from scratch
✔ Used the CF mapped route of incident-management-srv as the destination URL
✔ Understood how xs-app.json and manifest.json in the Fiori app reference this destination to route OData calls to the CAP backend

The destination is now the live bridge between the Fiori frontend and the CAP backend. In Part 6 , we will deploy the Fiori Elements app to the HTML5 Application Repository and access the complete end-to-end application.

entry.title: Michael Jackson: AI Cognitive Pipeline on SAP BTP, SAP CAP, ElevenLabs, Claude, HANA DB, Solace
entry.content:
Idea

Michael Jackson’s HIStory album opens with a five-minute spoken montage of real historical moments before asking “What about elephants? What about crying whales? What about killing fields?” The goal was to build a pipeline that listens in real time, identifies the historical events being referenced, retrieves real knowledge about each one from a knowledge base, builds an emotional arc across the full song, and generates a closing reflection at the end. This project is MJ Live.

Overview

MJ Live is a real-time AI cognitive pipeline deployed on SAP BTP. As the music plays, the system:

  • Transcribes audio in real time using ElevenLabs STT
  • Batches sentences and forwards them to SAP CAP
  • Runs a cognitive pipeline per transcript batch
  • Identifies historical events, emotions, figures and geographic coordinates using Claude Haiku
  • Retrieves relevant knowledge from a pre-embedded knowledge base using OpenAI embeddings and SAP HANA Cloud
  • Publishes each identified event to the consumer UI via Solace
  • Generates a closing reflection at the end of the song using Claude Opus

The result is a live display that fills up with real historical events as the music plays. Each event is mapped to the world, classified by emotion, and linked to documented history through RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation, the pattern where an AI query is matched against a pre-built knowledge base to retrieve relevant context before generating a response).

The entire pipeline runs on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry. SAP CAP handles the cognitive orchestration and OData layer, SAP HANA Cloud handles both event persistence and vector similarity search, and Solace delivers events to the consumer UI in real time. No external compute. No external vector database.

Demo

▶ YouTube: 

The video shows the full pipeline running on SAP BTP, from the first historical event in the HIStory speech through Earth Song and Man in the Mirror, ending with the AI’s closing reflection.

GitHub: User - shahidla. Repository - MJ

Design Flow

 

Architecture Diagram

Component

Technology

Role

Producer

Browser

Plays MJ.mp3, streams PCM audio

STT

ElevenLabs scribe_v2_realtime

Real-time speech-to-text, partials and finals

Bridge

Node.js on BTP CF

Sentence batching, WebSocket server, Solace publisher

Cognitive Pipeline

SAP CAP on BTP CF

Cognitive modes, session memory, event dedup

Event Classification

Claude Haiku (claude-haiku-4-5)

Historical event identification, emotion, figure, coordinates

Finale Reflection

Claude Opus (claude-opus-4-7)

Closing 3-sentence reflection at end of song

Embeddings

OpenAI text-embedding-3-small

Per-event query embedding for RAG

Knowledge Base

SAP HANA Cloud

86 historical events (1776 to 1997), pre-computed embeddings, cosine similarity search, native vector search on BTP, no external vector database

Messaging

Solace (SAP Event Broker)

Pub/sub from CAP to consumer UI

Consumer UI

Vanilla JS, CSS Grid, Canvas

World map, pipeline, chronicle, finale display

Main Flow

  1. Producer plays MJ.mp3 and streams PCM audio to the Bridge
  2. Bridge forwards audio to ElevenLabs via WebSocket
  3. ElevenLabs returns partial and final transcripts in real time
  4. Bridge extracts completed sentences, groups them in batches of 3 for context, and calls CAP receiveTranscript
  5. CAP runs the cognitive pipeline and returns identified events
  6. Bridge publishes each event to the consumer UI via Solace topics (chronicle/event, chronicle/finale, pipeline/status)
  7. Consumer UI renders the world map, chronicle, pipeline and emotion arc in real time
  8. When audio ends, the Bridge triggers generateFinale. CAP calls Claude Opus and publishes the closing reflection

Cognitive Modes

Modes 1 to 5 run per transcript batch. Modes 7 and 8 run once at the end of the song.

Mode 1  -> Transcript received, pipeline starts
Mode 2  -> Claude Haiku identifies events, emotions, figures, coordinates
Mode 3  -> Per-event RAG, each event gets its own targeted KB query
Mode 4  -> Temporal memory, builds context from what was witnessed
Mode 5  -> Relational reasoning, connects figures across events
Mode 6  -> Reflective evaluation (designed for between-act transitions, not implemented)
Mode 7  -> Pattern synthesis, finale preparation
Mode 8  -> Generative expression, Claude Opus writes the closing reflection

Technical Implementation

Sentence Batching

ElevenLabs delivers growing partials as it refines its transcription. Sending each sentence individually to Claude loses context. A short phrase like “Whatever I sing, that’s what I really mean” produces no historical event without surrounding context.

The Bridge accumulates 3 sentences before calling CAP. It also replaces earlier drafts of the same sentence with the corrected version using prefix matching, preventing the HIStory speech’s rapid date montage from filling the batch with intermediate STT corrections.

function addToBatch(text) {
  for (const sentence of extractSentences(clean)) {
    if (sentExact.has(sentence)) continue;
    sentExact.add(sentence);
    // Replace earlier draft of same sentence still in batch
    const prefix = sentence.substring(0, Math.min(15, sentence.length));
    const existingIdx = sentenceBatch.findIndex(s =>
      s.startsWith(prefix) || sentence.startsWith(s.substring(0, 15))
    );
    if (existingIdx >= 0) sentenceBatch[existingIdx] = sentence;
    else sentenceBatch.push(sentence);
  }
  if (sentenceBatch.length >= 3) {
    forwardToCAP(sentenceBatch.splice(0, 3).join(' '));
  }
}

Per-Event RAG

Each event identified by Claude gets its own embedding query. This prevents “What about elephants?” and “What about crying whales?” from competing in vector space when they appear in the same sentence.

const resultsWithRag = await Promise.all(results.map(async r => {
  const query = r.year
    ? [r.figure, r.year, r.event].filter(Boolean).join(' ')
    : transcript;
  const ragContext = await ragRetrieve(db, query);
  return { ...r, ragContext };
}));

A year boost (+0.15) is applied when the KB entry year matches a year explicitly spoken in the transcript:

const boost = transcriptYears.includes(kbYear) ? 0.15 : 0;
score = cosineSimilarity(queryVec, JSON.parse(embedding)) + boost;

The vector search runs entirely within SAP HANA Cloud on BTP. Embeddings are stored as pre-computed arrays in the knowledge base and ranked in JavaScript using cosine similarity. No external vector database is involved.

Event Dedup

The Bridge fires multiple CAP calls in parallel. A race condition occurs when two calls snapshot the dedup state before either registers. Both then persist the same event.

Solved with an inFlightKeys Set. After an event passes the dedup filter, its year and event-text prefix are registered immediately, before the async HANA persist, so concurrent calls see the key:

filtered.forEach(r => {
  if (r.year) {
    inFlightKeys.add(`${r.year}|${r.figure||''}`);
    inFlightKeys.add(r.year.toString());
    const ep = (r.event||'').toLowerCase().trim().substring(0, 20);
    if (ep) inFlightKeys.add(`evt:${r.year}:${ep}`);
  }
});

Finale — Claude Opus

The top_p parameter is deprecated on claude-opus-4-7. LangChain sends -1 as a default, causing the call to fail. The finale uses the Anthropic SDK directly:

const response = await client.messages.create({
  model: 'claude-opus-4-7',
  max_tokens: 1024,
  messages: [{ role: 'user', content: prompt }]
});

The prompt does not prescribe a narrative arc. It gives Claude the raw list of witnessed events and emotional journey, and asks it to find its own thread in exactly 3 standalone sentences.

The system is not specific to Michael Jackson. Any audio that produces historical events through the STT pipeline will generate a corresponding finale. Play the same song twice and the reflection will differ. Play a different song and the AI witnesses different moments, builds a different emotional arc, and asks a different closing question.

Consumer UI

 

Consumer Overview

The display has three sections:

Left column — AI Cognitive Pipeline Each mode lights up in gold as the pipeline processes a transcript. In the screenshot below, RAG RETRIEVAL is active and the system has just identified Rosa Parks, 1955. The last result stays visible between calls so the screen is never blank.

 

Pipeline Active

Right column — The Chronicle Each identified event appears as a card with the year in large type, the event description, the figure involved, and below it in blue the KB entry retrieved via vector search. In the screenshot below you can see Yuri Gagarin (1961), RFK (1968) and Thomas Edison (1877), each matched to the correct knowledge base entry.

 

Chronicle Entries

Bottom — Audio Signal + Emotional Arc The EQ visualiser shows the live audio signal from the music. The emotion arc builds as a bar chart across the session, showing how the emotional distribution shifts from wonder to anger to grief as the songs progress.

World map: every event with geographic coordinates gets a glowing red dot plotted at its location. As the song plays, the map fills up across continents.

Log Page

 

Log Events

Every event is persisted to SAP HANA. The log page shows the full pipeline output for each event: the transcript heard, Claude’s classification, and all 4 KB entries retrieved ranked by cosine similarity.

The CAP Calls tab shows every transcript batch that reached the pipeline, with timestamp and status.

 

CAP Calls

Result

From the BTP demo run: 30 events, no errors, finale generated.

Year

Event

RAG Match

1827

Beethoven dies

Beethoven KB entry ✓

1929

Wall Street Crash

Wall Street KB entry ✓

1927

Lindbergh transatlantic flight

Lindbergh KB entry ✓

1942

Muhammad Ali born

Ali KB entry ✓

1947

Chuck Yeager breaks sound barrier

Yeager KB entry ✓

1955

Rosa Parks refuses her bus seat

Rosa Parks KB entry ✓

1961

Yuri Gagarin first human in space

Gagarin KB entry ✓

1969

Apollo 11 moon landing

Armstrong KB entry ✓

1968

MLK assassination + RFK speech

Both KB entries ✓

1975

Cambodian genocide

Cambodia KB entry ✓

1986

African elephant poaching crisis

Elephant KB entry ✓

1992

Somalia famine

Somalia KB entry ✓

Finale — Claude Opus:

After the last note, the system calls Claude Opus with every event witnessed across the full song and asks it to write a closing reflection in 3 sentences with no prescribed structure. The reflection below is from the BTP demo run.

 

Finale Screen

I used to think witnessing was a passive thing, a kind of standing still while history moved through me. But the desert taught me what the mirror confirmed: that seeing without returning is just another form of looking away. So tell me — after everything I have watched rise and fall and rise again, what good is a witness who never goes back to change the man in the glass?

What This Shows

This is a prototype, but it is deployed to SAP BTP Cloud Foundry and ran live against real audio. Claude Haiku had no pre-programmed answers. It heard a sentence batch, placed events in time and geography, and was right. Consistently. The temporal memory across the session means each event is heard in the context of everything before it, not in isolation. The finale prompt gives Claude no template and no narrative. Yet it found a thread connecting figures and crises from different decades. No rule-based system produces that finale. Rules repeat what they were told. Claude found a question nobody gave it. The pipeline is straightforward. What the model does inside it is not.

The AI does not know it is listening to Michael Jackson. It just knows what it heard, and it tells you what it understood.

What makes it deployable at this level is the SAP BTP stack: SAP CAP as the service and orchestration layer, SAP HANA Cloud as the persistence and vector search backend, and Solace for real-time event delivery. All of it runs on Cloud Foundry with a standard cf push. No custom infrastructure. No external vector database. The platform handles the plumbing so the AI work stays in focus.

References

  • SAP CAP Documentation
  • ElevenLabs Scribe v2 Realtime
  • Anthropic Claude API
  • OpenAI Embeddings
  • Solace PubSub+
  • SAP HANA Cloud
  • GitHub: User - shahidla. Repository - MJ

This blog post was drafted with the assistance of Claude (Anthropic).

Conclusion

MJ Live shows what happens when you compose AI services correctly rather than use them in isolation. Each design decision came from a specific failure encountered during development: sentence batching fixed the context problem, per-event RAG fixed the vector competition, and inFlightKeys fixed the parallel call race.

The pipeline produced 30 historical events from a 6-minute song play, with correct RAG matches throughout and a closing reflection the AI wrote without a template. What was unexpected was not that the AI identified Rosa Parks or Neil Armstrong. It was that when given only the raw events and no instructions, it asked a question none of us scripted.

The pipeline is not built around Michael Jackson specifically. The knowledge base, the cognitive modes, and the finale prompt are all content-agnostic. MJ Live is the demo. The pipeline is the point.

The source code is on GitHub.

The AI asked a question nobody scripted. That is the result worth keeping.

Disclaimer

This is a prototype created for learning and demonstration purposes, but it is deployed to SAP BTP Cloud Foundry and ran live against real audio. Functional gaps and bugs may exist. SAP guidelines, best practices, coding standards, authorisation requirements and performance considerations were not in scope. A real solution would require proper error handling, security checks, resilience measures and alignment with enterprise development standards.

entry.title: Como integrar um serviço BTP Cloud Foundry a um ambiente S4HANA On-premise
entry.content:
O cenário é o seguinte: você quer hospedar um app Fiori Freestyle no BTP Cloud Foundry, que vai se conectar a um sistema S4HANA on-premise e será utilizado pelos usuários finais.

Pode parecer bem específico, mas isso envolve um entendimento de várias camadas de arquitetura, então vale a pena tomar um tempo para entender isso.

Esse objetivo envolve criar serviços auxiliares, que vão se comunicar com o S4HANA por intermédio do Cloud Connector.

O primeiro passo é instalar o SAP Cloud Connector, pode ser no mesmo servidor que hospeda o S4HANA ou em outro servidor na mesma rede.

Ele é um programa Java, então é necessário instalar a JVM. Tecnicamente pode ser usada qualquer JVM, porém a SAP também possui suas próprias versões da JVM, então é recomendável usar elas. Os detalhes estão nesse link: https://help.sap.com/docs/connectivity/sap-btp-connectivity-cf/installation.

Com ele rodando no ambiente, você vai configurar os dados da sua BTP subaccount (ele pode se conectar em mais de uma subaccount).

A conexão entre os serviços da subaccount e o S4HANA vão ser intermediadas por essa conexão com o Cloud Connector.

Quando falamos em integração de serviços externos com um ambiente SAP, não podemos ignorar a parte de autenticação e segurança. Existem várias formas de configuração, mas a mais “nativa” para esse cenário seria a Principal Propagation.

Para entender a arquitetura, mentalize o seguinte fluxo, supondo que no Cloud Foundry você escolha o Node como backend:

  • O usuário abre a aplicação Fiori, que tenta puxar uma informação de uma API do S4HANA através do path relativo (Ex: /sap/opu/odata4/sap/z*);

  • Essa requisição cai no container Node, e se o usuário não estiver logado, ele é redirecionado para uma url de autenticação, onde é informado seu email e senha do Identity Provider (que pode ser da SAP, Azure, AWS, etc.);

  • Essa autenticação gera um token com informações do usuário, que é enviado ao Node;

  • O Node, tendo o token e o caminho da API, direciona ambos para um destination, que aponta para a conexão criada no Cloud Connector;

  • O Cloud Connector pega uma informação desse token, por exemplo o e-mail do usuário, e gera um certificado digital temporário;

  • Esse certificado é enviado em uma conexão https para o endpoint real do S4HANA

  • O S4HANA valida o certificado e mapeia ele para um usuário interno

  • A partir daí a lógica da API odata segue normal, baseada no que o usuário pode fazer, e a resposta é devolvida;

Existe um outro blog post que também explica sobre o Principal Propagation. De qualquer forma vou explicar aqui, mas se quiser conferir, o link é: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/principal-propagation-setup-between-s4hana-and-btp-portal/ba-p/13522753.

Para tornar isso possível, após conectar o Cloud Connector na subaccount, o próximo passo vai ser configurar os certificados digitais dele, na área “Connector > Configuration > On-premises”.

Nesse painel existem 2 tipos de certificado digital: um é para o proxy do Cloud Connector, esse é denominado “System certificate”, e o outro é o que o Cloud Connector utiliza para gerar os certificados temporários, denominado “CA Certificate”.

Nessa mesma página é possível configurar quais informações extrair do token para gerar o certificado, na área do Principal Propagation.

Após isso, é hora de configurar a conexão com o S4HANA on-premise.

Primeiramente, o Cloud Connector precisa confiar no certificado https do servidor S4HANA. Por padrão ele só confia em uma lista definida de certificados, denominada “Back-end Trust Store”. Você pode fazer lá o upload manualmente, ou pode também desativar essa opção, assim ele vai confiar em qualquer certificado do back-end.

Feito isso, na área da sua subaccount, página “Cloud to On-Premises”, vai ser configurado os detalhes específicos do proxy.

A conexão deve ser necessariamente via HTTPS, pois é assim que o Cloud Connector consegue trocar o certificado gerado com o S4HANA de forma nativa através do próprio protocolo.

Exemplo (na edição ele exibe como HTTP, mas foi criada como HTTPS):

Feito isso, a próxima etapa é fazer o Cloud Connector confiar no token gerado pelo serviço de autenticação da sua subaccount.

Para isso, nessa mesma página do “Cloud to On-Premises” existe também a aba do “Principal propagation”, onde você deve sincronizar. A primeira execução é manual, depois dela você pode marcar a opção para sincronizar automaticamente.

 

Em resumo, até aqui já temos uma instância do Cloud Connector rodando na rede, vinculada a uma subaccount BTP, com os certificados system e CA criados, e configurada para se conectar ao S4HANA on-premise via https.

Agora faltam 2 coisas: configurar o S4HANA para confiar no Cloud Connector, e configurar os serviços no BTP Cloud Foundry para que seja possível essa integração.

Para que o S4HANA confie no Cloud Connector, é necessário ajustar alguns parâmetros de perfil do sistema (isso envolve configurações BASIS).

O primeiro ponto é fazer ele confiar no certificado digital de sistema do Cloud Connector, existem 2 formas de fazer isso:

  • Com os antigos parâmetros “icm/HTTPS/trust_client_with_subject” e “icm/HTTPS/trust_client_with_issuer”, que são configurados via transação RZ10. Esses parâmetros definem o padrão do emissor e assunto do certificado digital que o sistema pode confiar, e é um valor único. Eles são do tipo estático, então é necessário reiniciar a instância do S4 para aplicar as edições.

Exemplo:

  • Com o novo parâmetro “icm/trusted_reverse_proxy_<x>”, onde o “<x>” é um número sequencial começando do 0. Ele tem esse padrão porque dessa forma é possível configurar mais de um certificado confiado pelo servidor, diferente dos anteriores que só permitiam um valor. Ele é do tipo dinâmico, então não é sempre necessário reiniciar a instância para aplicar, e pode ser alterado a partir da transação RZ11 (de forma indireta). Mais informações sobre ele no link: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_NETWEAVER_AS_ABAP_752/077f95b579a0474fbef5d520be10b680/3bea0adde9a54db5843be8f7bf01fefa.html.

Exemplo:

Também é necessário importar os dois certificados do Cloud Connector na transação STRUST, no servidor SSL Padrão.

Feito isso, o sistema S4HANA confia no certificado do Cloud Connector, mas ainda não consegue mapear os certificados temporários para usuários dentro do SAP. Aí entra a configuração da transação CERTRULE, onde você faz o upload de um certificado temporário gerado pelo Cloud Connector e pode mapear ele para usuários através de regras, ou diretamente.

Exemplo:

Para habilitar isso também é necessário configurar um parâmetro de perfil, o “login/certificate_mapping_rulebased”:

No lado do S4HANA é basicamente isso, agora só falta configurar os serviços dentro do BTP.

O cenário pensado nesse tutorial é de uma aplicação CAP Fiori rodando no Cloud Foudry, então vamos criar os serviços no mesmo space da aplicação.

São basicamente 3 serviços:

  • destination: Conceito similar ao da transação SM59, guarda dentro dele o destino para a conexão do Cloud Connector. Ele possui painel próprio para configurações.

  • connectivity: É um serviço sem configuração nenhuma, que apenas serve como ponte entre a aplicação e o serviço de destination.

  • xsuaa: É o que faz a ponte da aplicação com o provedor de identidade. Ele é configurado apenas via json, onde é possível definir o app que vai consumi-lo, as urls que pode redirecionar após autenticar o usuário, e até mesmo criar novas roles dentro do BTP, que podem posteriormente ser incluídas em Role collections e atribuídas a usuários.

Feito isso, só falta configurar a conexão dentro do painel do “Destination Service”. Lá você vai criar um destination com o Proxy type “OnPremise” e mapear para a url virtual gerada no painel do Cloud Connector, com autenticação via “PrincipalPropagation”.

Exemplo:

O app node deve estar configurado para que o destino do S4HANA seja esse Name que você configurou acima (não confunda isso com a url virtual ou com o serviço destination).

Agora para integrar tudo isso, basta a aplicação estar configurada para fazer o binding nesses 3 serviços. Isso é feito através do arquivo “manifest.yml”, usado na hora de subir o app no Cloud Foundry.

Exemplo:

Finalmente, seu app Node estará pronto para se autenticar e consumir serviços odata do seu S4HANA On-Premise.

 

Em resumo, ao seguir esse tutorial você foi capaz de entender e configurar as camadas de integração entre um app no BTP Cloud Foundry e um sistema S4Hana On-Premise (via api OData).

Talvez essas ferramentas, configurações e caminhos específicos mudem com o tempo, o mais importante é entender a arquitetura e os conceitos envolvidos nessa integração.

Obrigado pela leitura, espero ter ajudado 😄

entry.title: From Concept to action: Hands-On with the CAP Operator and the Partner Reference Application
entry.content:
Introduction

In my recent blog series, Kyma Evolution: Transforming SAP Kyma into a Tailor-Made SaaS Platform for CAP, I explored the architecture behind turning SAP BTP, Kyma runtime into a native, clean-core-compliant foundation for multitenant Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) applications. We discussed the concept of shifting heavy operational workloads—like tenant provisioning, container routing, and database migration—away from custom application logic and directly into the Kubernetes control plane via the CAP Operator.

Concepts and architecture diagrams are crucial, but as developers, we truly understand a framework when we see it working in real code.

To bridge the gap between architectural theory and practical deployment, I have created a hands-on technical walkthrough. I took the standard SAP-provided Partner Reference Application (PRA)—the gold standard sample for complex BTP multitenant SaaS engineering—and forked it to demonstrate exactly how to transition a standard CAP application into a declarative, CAP Operator-managed deployment.

You can find the complete implementation and step-by-step repository here: 👉ElectronicWizzard/partner-reference-application (kymapra1 branch) 

Why the Partner Reference Application?

The Partner Reference Application is designed to showcase enterprise-grade SaaS development on SAP BTP. It contains complex real-world challenges:

  • Multitenancy with isolated data access.
  • Integration with BTP core services (XSUAA, Destination, SaaS Provisioning Service).
  • Explicit lifecycle tasks for tenant onboarding and offboarding.

By migrating this specific application to the CAP Operator, we demonstrate that the operator isn't just for simple "Hello World" microservices. It is fully capable of managing sophisticated, production-grade enterprise architectures.

Here is a glimpse of the migration workflow detailed in the hands-on guide:

1. Project Preparation & Plugin Integration

Instead of writing complex Kubernetes deployment manifests from scratch, we leverage the tooling built into the CAP ecosystem. By injecting the development dependency into the project root:

npm add @cap-js/cap-operator-plugin -D

We unlock the plugin capabilities that assist in automatically deriving the necessary configuration files and container patterns directly from our existing cds models and configuration schema.

2. Containerization and Registry Push

As container-native deployments are standard practice, the workflow guides you through leveraging Docker to containerize the CAP application services and push them to a container registry accessible by your Kyma cluster.

3. Transitioning to Custom Resources (CRDs)

The heart of the transformation lies in replacing standard Kubernetes Deployments and StatefulSets with custom resources defined by the CAP Operator, such as CAPApplication.

Instead of writing custom logic to handle mtxs (multitenancy/extensibility) operations during tenant registration, you simply declare the application's characteristics in a clean, unified manifest. The CAP Operator watches this resource and automatically sets up:

  • The necessary application pods.
  • Automated routing rules.
  • Secure BTP Services integration using the SAP BTP Service Operator.
  • Automated tenant provisioning
  • ...

Prerequisites to Get Started

To follow along with the tutorial and deploy the project yourself, make sure you have the following environments prepared:

  1. A Kyma Cluster with the CAP Operator Installed: Follow the setup detailed in the Kyma Evolution series to ensure your cluster control plane is ready to interpret the custom CAP resources.

  2. BTP Provider Subaccount: A dedicated provider subaccount mapped to your Kyma namespace and entitlement of the required services.

  3. Local Tooling: Docker installed on your machine and access to a container registry (e.g., Docker Hub, GitHub Packages).

Conclusion & Next Steps

Shifting from imperative scripting to a declarative, operator-driven cloud runtime is a massive step forward for building resilient, clean-core SaaS applications on SAP BTP. By analyzing the changes in this fork, you will see exactly how much boilerplate code and deployment complexity can be stripped away from your project lifecycle.

Head over to the repository, check out the kymapra1 branch, and walk through the implementation: 🔗 Read the Full Deployment Guide on GitHub

For those who want to start with there own workload I would like to recommend the new SAP Tutorial for the CAP Operator on the Kyma Environment.

I would love to hear your thoughts! Have you started experimenting with the CAP Operator in your own Kyma or Kubernetes environments? What challenges or successes have you encountered during the shift to declarative operations? Let's discuss in the comments below.

entry.title: How to Restrict CRUD Operations in SAP CAPM Using @Capabilities, @readonly and Test APIs with .http
entry.content:
Hi Everyone, 

Today, I would like to share a blog about restricting CRUD operations in SAP CAPM using '@Capabilities' and '@readonly' annotations and testing APIs with .http files. 

In SAP CAPM, entities are automatically exposed as OData services with CRUD operations. However, in many business scenarios, developers need to control which operations users are allowed to perform on specific entities. 

Using '@Capabilities', we can control Insert, Update, and Delete operations individually for entities. Using '@readonly', we can make an entity completely read-only by allowing only GET operations. These annotations help protect business data from unwanted modifications and improve API security. 

Below, I’ll guide you through a simple step-by-step process to implement CRUD restrictions using '@Capabilities' and '@readonly', and test APIs directly inside BAS using .http files. 

Overview 

CRUD stands for: 

Create → POST  
Read → GET  
Update → PATCH / PUT  
Delete → DELETE  
By default, SAP CAPM allows all CRUD operations for entities exposed through services. 

In real-world applications, this can create issues such as: 

Users accidentally deleting records  
Unauthorized updates to important data  
Business rules requiring read-only entities  
To solve this, SAP CAPM provides the '@Capabilities' and '@readonly' annotation. 

Using this annotation, developers can: 

Allow or restrict insert operations  
Allow or restrict update operations  
Allow or restrict delete operations  
Make entities completely read-only using '@readonly'  
Protect business 
For demonstration purposes, I am using an employee entity, for example. 

Step 1: Create Employee Entity 


Open the following file: 

db/datamodel.cds 

Add the following Employee entity: 

namespace dip.db; 
using { cuid , Currency} from '@sap/cds/common'; 
using { dip.common } from './common'; 

type Guid : String(32); 

context master { 
entity employee : cuid
{ 
    nameFirst: String(40); 
    nameMiddle: String(40); 
    nameLast: String(40); 
    nameInitials: String(40); 
    sex : common.Gender; 
    language: String(5); 
    phoneNumber: common.PhoneNumber; 
    email: common.Email; 
    loginName: String(20); 
    Currency: Currency; 
    salaryAmount: common.AmountT; 
    accountNumber: String(16); 
    bankId: String(8); 
    bankName: String(64); 
    validFrom: Date; 
    validTo: Date; 
} 
}
 
 Step 2: Expose Entity in Service Layer 

Open the following file: 

srv/catalogService.cds 

Add the following code: 

using { dip.db.master } from '../db/datamodel'; 
service CatalogService  @(path:'/CatalogService')  

{    
    entity EmployeeSet as projection on master.employee;
}
 
Step 3: Create Common Types File 

Before exposing the entity in the service layer, we will create a reusable common file for shared data types and validations. 

In SAP CAPM, common.cds is used to define reusable: 

  • custom types  
  • validations  
  • enums  
  • aspects  

This helps reduce duplicate code and improves project maintainability. 

Open the following file:

db/common.cds

Add the following code: 

namespace dip.common; 
using { } from '@sap/cds/common'; 

type Gender : String(20) enum 
{ 
  Male          = 'M'; 
  Female        = 'F'; 
  nonBinary     = 'N'; 
  noDisclosure  = 'D'; 
  selfDescribe  = 'S'; 
}; 

type AmountT : Decimal(15, 2) @( 
  Semantics.amount.currencyCode : 'CURRENCY_CODE', 
  sap.units                     : 'CURRENCY_CODE' 
); 

aspect Amount { 
  CURRENCY_CODE : String(4); 
  GROSS_AMOUNT  : AmountT; 
  NET_AMOUNT    : AmountT; 
  TAX_AMOUNT    : AmountT; 
} 

type PhoneNumber : String(14) 
  @assert.format.pattern : '((?:\\+|00)[17](?: |\\-)?|(?:\\+|00)[1-9]\\d{0,2}(?: |\\-)?|(?:\\+|00)1\\-\\d{3}(?: |\\-)?)(0\\d|([0-9]{3})|[1-9]{0,3})(?:((?: |\\-)[0-9]{2}){4}|((?:[0-9]{2}){4})|((?: |\\-)[0-9]{3}(?: |\\-)[0-9]{4})|([0-9]{7}))'; 

type Email : String(255) 
  @assert.format.pattern : '^([a-zA-Z0-9_\\-\\.]+)@([a-zA-Z0-9_\\-\\.]+)\\.([a-zA-Z]{2,5})$';
 
Step 4: Run the CAPM Application 

After creating the entity, service layer, and common types file, the next step is to run the SAP CAPM application. 

Open the terminal in SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) and execute the following command: 

cds watch

The cds watch command starts the CAPM server and automatically reloads the application whenever changes are made in CDS files or service files. 

Once the application starts successfully, you will see the server running on: 

http://localhost:4004 

You can now access the OData services from the browser. 

 

Step 5: Open OData Service

Open the following URL in browser: 

localhost:4004/CatalogService/ 
You will see the available entity sets including: 

EmployeeSet 

 
Step 6: Create. http File for API Testing 

In SAP Business Application Studio (BAS), create a new file named: 

test.http 

The .http file is used to test APIs directly inside BAS without external tools like Postman. 

Add the following requests inside test.http: 

### employeeSet top 5 
GET http://localhost:4004/CatalogService/EmployeeSet?$top=5 

### INSERT Employee 
POST http://localhost:4004/CatalogService/EmployeeSet 
Content-Type: application/json 
{ 
  "nameFirst": "Dip", 
  "nameMiddle": "Ramesh", 
  "nameLast": "Ragho", 
  "nameInitials": "DRR", 
  "sex": "F", 
  "language": "en", 
  "phoneNumber": "9876500001", 
  "email": "raj1@gmail.com", 
  "loginName": "rajkumar1", 
  "Currency_code": "INR", 
  "salaryAmount": 56000, 
  "accountNumber": "123456789001", 
  "bankId": "CBI001", 
  "bankName": "Central Bank", 
  "validFrom": "2024-01-01", 
  "validTo": "2030-12-31" 
} 

### UPDATE Employee 
PATCH http://localhost:4004/CatalogService/EmployeeSet('111e8400-e29b-41d4-a716-446655440001') 
Content-Type: application/json   

{ 
  "salaryAmount": 56000 
} 

### DELETE Employee 
DELETE http://localhost:4004/CatalogService/EmployeeSet('111e8400-e29b-41d4-a716-446655440001')

Using .http files, developers can: 

  • test CRUD operations  
  • verify API responses  
  • test restrictions from '@Capabilities'  
  • test read-only behavior from '@readonly'

directly inside BAS. 

Run the POST request from the .http file to insert employee data. 

 
Step 7: Apply CRUD Restrictions Using '@readonly' and '@Capabilities'

After successfully testing the APIs, the next step is to apply CRUD restrictions on the EmployeeSet entity using '@readonly' and '@Capabilities' annotations. 

These annotations help control which operations users are allowed to perform on the entity. 

Using '@readonly'

The '@readonly' annotation makes the entity completely read-only. 

Only the GET operation is allowed. 
POST, PATCH, and DELETE operations are automatically restricted. 

Open the following file: 

srv/catalogService.cds 

Update the code as shown below: 

using { dip.db.master } from '../db/datamodel'; 
service CatalogService  @(path:'/CatalogService') 

{  
    @readonly 
    entity EmployeeSet as projection on master.employee; 
}

Testing '@readonly'

Now run the same API requests from the .http file. 

You will notice: 

  • GET request works successfully  
  • POST request fails  
  • PATCH request fails  
  • DELETE request fails  

Expected error response: 

405 Method Not Allowed 

Expected Output 

Below you can see the screenshots of the API responses generated during testing. 

successful GET response  

 

 failed POST response

 

failed PATCH response

 

failed DELETE response 

 
 Using '@Capabilities'

The '@Capabilities' annotation provides more flexibility by allowing developers to control Insert, Update, and Delete operations individually. 

Open the following file: 

srv/catalogService.cds 

Update the code as shown below: 

using { dip.db.master } from '../db/datamodel'; 

service CatalogService  @(path:'/CatalogService') {  

   @Capabilities : { 
   Insertable  : true, 
   Updatable   : false, 
   Deletable   : false 
} 
    entity EmployeeSet as projection on master.employee; 
}

Allowed and Restricted Operations 

Operation 

Status 

GET 

Allowed 

POST 

Allowed 

PATCH 

Restricted 

DELETE 

Restricted 

Testing '@Capabilities' 

Run the API requests again using the .http file. 

You will notice: 

  • GET request works  
  • POST request works  
  • PATCH request fails  
  • DELETE request fails  

Expected restricted response: 

405 Method Not Allowed 

Expected Output 

Below you can see the screenshots of the API responses generated during testing. 

GET success response  

 

POST success response  

 

PATCH restricted response

 

DELETE restricted response 

 

Conclusion 

In this blog, we learned how to restrict CRUD operations in SAP CAPM using '@Capabilities' and '@readonly' annotations. We also explored how to test APIs directly inside SAP Business Application Studio using .http files. 

These annotations help developers secure APIs, control user operations, and protect business data from unwanted modifications. 

Thank you for reading this blog. I hope this blog helps you understand CRUD restriction concepts in SAP CAPM more effectively.

 

Thanks and Regards, 

Deepa Ramesh Ragho

entry.title: Deep Insert in SAP CAPM Using Composition and Fiori Elements
entry.content:
Introduction 

In enterprise applications, it is very common to create a parent record together with one or more child records in a single transaction. For example, while creating a department, users may also want to maintain all Employees belonging to that department at the same time. 

In SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAPM), this requirement is handled through Deep Insert. 

Deep Insert allows us to send a single OData request containing: 

  • Parent entity data  
  • Related child entity data  

CAP automatically processes the complete payload and inserts all records in one transaction. 

In this blog, we will implement Deep Insert using the following entities: 

  • Department as the parent entity  
  • Employee as the child entity  
  • Composition to define the parent-child relationship  
  • Service projections in srv/emp.cds  
  • Fiori Elements annotations in app/annotations.cds 

 

Business Scenario 

Assume we are developing an HR application. 

When a user creates a new Department, they should also be able to add multiple Employees within the same Create page. 

For example: 

Department Details 

  • Name: Information Technology  
  • Manager Name: Hari Kumar  
  • Location: Bangalore  

Employees 

  • John Doe  
  • Jane Smith  
  • David Miller  

When the user clicks Save, both the Department and all Employee records should be inserted automatically. 

 

Data Model Design 

The data model consists of two entities: 

  1. Department  
  1. Employee  

The Department entity contains a composition named employees, which establishes a strong parent-child relationship. 

This is the key requirement for Deep Insert. 

 

Db/datamodel.cds: 

Department entity:

namespace harikumar.db;

entity Department {
    key ID              : UUID;
    NAME                : String;
    description         : String;
    location            : String;
    managerName         : String;
    managerEmail        : String;
    budget              : Decimal(15,2);
    employeeCount       : Integer;
    establishedDate     : Date;
    departmentCode      : String(10);
    status              : String;
    phoneNumber         : String;
    costCenter          : String;
    floorNumber         : Integer;
    buildingName        : String;
    employees           : Composition of many Employee on employees.department = $self;
}

employees : Composition of many Employee on employees.department = $self; 

  • This statement is the most important part of the implementation. 

Meaning of This Statement 

  • Composition defines a parent-child relationship.  
  • many Employee means one department can have multiple employees.  
  • employees.department = $self links each employee back to the current department.  
  • Child records are created, updated, and deleted together with the parent. 

Why Composition is Important 

Deep Insert works automatically only when child entities are modeled using Composition. 

If you use only Association, CAP will not automatically create child records during the same request. 

Employee Entity: 

entity Employee {
    key ID              : UUID;
    nameFirst           : String;
    nameMiddle          : String;
    nameLast            : String;
    nameInitial         : String;
    gender              : String;
    validFrom           : Timestamp;
    validTo             : Date;
    language            : String;
    phoneNumber         : String;
    email               : String;
    loginName           : String;
    currency_code       : Integer;
    salaryAmount        : Integer;
    accountNumber       : String;
    bankId              : String;
    bankName            : String;
    status              : String;
    department          : Association to Department;
}

 

department : Association to Department; 

  • This association stores the foreign key reference to the parent department. 
  • Each employee belongs to exactly one department 

 

Service Layer Implementation (srv/emp.cds) 

The service layer exposes the database entities as OData services 

using {harikumar.db as db} from '../db/datamodel';
service CatalogService @(path:'/CatalogService') {
    @odata.draft.enabled:true
     entity departmentSet as projection on db.Department{
        *,
        employees
     };
    entity employeeSet as projection on db.Employee;  
}

@odata.draft.enabled : true 

Enables draft support for Fiori Elements. 

Benefits: 

  • Save as draft  
  • Resume editing later  
  • Transactional consistency 

 

entity departmentSet as projection on db.Department { 

*, 

employees 

}; 

Why employees Must Be Included 

This projection exposes the composition to the service. 

Without this line: 

  • The Employees section will not appear in the Object Page.  
  • Deep Insert payload will not contain child entities.  
  • CAP cannot process nested data. 

app/project1/annotations.cds:

using CatalogService as service from '../../srv/emp';
annotate service.departmentSet with @(
    UI.LineItem:[
        {
            $Type:'UI.DataField',
            Label:'Name',
            Value:NAME
        },
        {
            $Type:'UI.DataField',
            Label:'Manager Name',
            Value:managerName
        },
        {
            $Type:'UI.DataField',
            Label:'Manager Email',
            Value:managerEmail
        },
        {
            $Type:'UI.DataField',
            Label:'Phone Number',
            Value:phoneNumber
        },
        {
            $Type:'UI.DataField',
            Label:'Status',
            Value:status
        },
    ],
    UI.FieldGroup #DepartmentDetails : {
        $Type : 'UI.FieldGroupType',
        Data : [
            {
                $Type : 'UI.DataField',
                Label : 'Name',
                Value : NAME
            },
            {
                $Type : 'UI.DataField',
                Label : 'Description',
                Value : description
            },
            {
                $Type : 'UI.DataField',
                Label : 'Location',
                Value : location
            },
            {
                $Type : 'UI.DataField',
                Label : 'Manager Name',
                Value : managerName
            },
            {
                $Type : 'UI.DataField',
